Florida AG to probe OpenAI, alleging possible connection to FSU shooting
articles.description: Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier plans to investigate OpenAI for its alleged harm to minors, potential to threaten national security, and its possible link to a shooting at Florida State University last year.

articles.title: ChatGPT finally offers $100/month Pro plan
articles.description: OpenAI announced on Thursday something that power users have been asking for: a $100/month plan. Previously, subscriptions jumped from $20 to $200 per month.

articles.title: EFF is the latest organization to leave X
articles.description: The EFF follows the departure of various news organizations and others who no longer find X a viable source of traffic.

articles.title: What founders can learn from Anjuna’s layoffs and recovery
articles.description: In 2021, Anjuna Security was growing fast, hiring aggressively, and chasing a market that seemed limitless. By the end of that year, the venture-backed cybersecurity company had scaled to around 75 employees, building out sales, customer success, and support teams in anticipation of continued hyperg

articles.title: Volkswagen drops all-electric ID.4 in the US in pivot back to gas SUVs
articles.description: All resources at its U.S. factory are aimed at the upcoming Atlas SUV.

articles.title: Florida AG announces investigation into OpenAI over shooting that allegedly involved ChatGPT
articles.description: ChatGPT had reportedly been used to plan the attack that killed two and injured five at Florida State University last April. The family of one victim has said that they plan to sue OpenAI over the incident.

articles.title: StubHub to pay $10M to settle FTC allegations over ‘deceptive’ ticket pricing
articles.description: The consumer protection agency says the company violated the FTC Act and its Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Fees by "deceptively" advertising ticket prices on its website without clearly disclosing upfront what the total cost would be, including all mandatory fees.

articles.title: After data breach, $10B-valued startup Mercor is having a month
articles.description: After falling victim to a hacker, Mercor is facing lawsuits and reportedly losing big-name customers.

articles.title: Meta AI app climbs to No. 5 on the App Store after Muse Spark launch
articles.description: The app was ranking No. 57 on the App Store just before Meta AI's new model launched. Now it's No. 5 — and rising.

articles.title: Is Anthropic limiting the release of Mythos to protect the internet — or Anthropic?
articles.description: Anthropic said this week that it limited the release of its newest model, dubbed Mythos, because it is too capable of finding security exploits in software relied upon by users around the world. Are real cybersecurity concerns a cover for a bigger problem at the frontier lab?

articles.title: Google and Intel deepen AI infrastructure partnership
articles.description: The two tech giants are looking to co-develop custom chips, at a time when demand for CPUs is high due to a growing global shortage.

articles.title: How to make the Startup Battlefield Top 20 — and what every company gets regardless
articles.description: Every founder who applies to Startup Battlefield wants the same thing: the Disrupt Main Stage. Here’s how to get there and why the opportunity starts well before the main stage.

articles.title: You can now edit your Instagram comments
articles.description: Instagram users can now edit comments, which is a long-awaited fix for anyone who’s ever had to delete and re-post a comment just to fix a typo.

articles.title: Sierra’s Bret Taylor says the era of clicking buttons is over
articles.description: Last month, Sierra launched Ghostwriter, an agent designed to build other agents. With this “agent as a service” tool, the startup intends to replace traditional click-based web applications with natural language. Users simply describe what they need, prompting Ghostwriter to autonomously create and

articles.title: Avec’s Tinder-style email app allows you to swipe through your inbox
articles.description: Users can use the in-built voice transcription tool to reply to emails

articles.title: X brings back Voice Notes to X Chat
articles.description: X Chat brings back Voice Notes to DMs and group chats after removing the option during the upgrade.

articles.title: Hacker stole £700,000 from UK energy company by redirecting payment
articles.description: The U.K. energy company said a redirected payment meant for a contractor instead landed in a hacker's bank account.

articles.title: Amazon CEO takes aim at Nvidia, Intel, Starlink, more in annual shareholder letter
articles.description: Andy Jassy's annual shareholder letter reads something like a diss track to a wide range of competitors as he defends spending $200 billion in capex.

articles.title: Final 2 days to save up to $500 on your TechCrunch Disrupt 2026 ticket
articles.description: Ticket discounts of up to $500 will end tomorrow, April 10, at 11:59 p.m. PT. After that, prices for TechCrunch Disrupt 2026 go up again. Miss this, and you’ll be paying more for the same access to one of the most anticipated tech events of the year. Register now to lock in these savings.

articles.title: Spotify now lets everyone turn off videos in its app
articles.description: The controls can be used to choose an audio-first experience on the app or a video-enhanced one, Spotify says.

digest:
## 🔬 Major Technical Developments

### How NASA built Artemis II's fault-tolerant computer
URL: https://cacm.acm.org/news/how-nasa-built-artemis-iis-fault-tolerant-computer/
Radiation-hardened computing for human spaceflight returns after 50 years
NASA's approach to Artemis II computing reveals the engineering trade-offs between COTS components and mission-critical reliability. The architecture choices here influence how defense contractors and aerospace suppliers approach fault tolerance—watch for downstream adoption in satellite constellations and autonomous systems.

### Reverse engineering Gemini's SynthID detection
URL: https://github.com/aloshdenny/reverse-SynthID
Google's AI watermarking scheme may be more fragile than advertised
This reverse engineering effort exposes potential weaknesses in SynthID, Google's approach to watermarking AI-generated content. Regulatory frameworks assuming reliable AI detection (EU AI Act, state legislation) may need reassessment if watermarking proves trivially defeatable.

### A new world for security-critical projects
URL: https://bytecodealliance.org/articles/wasmtime-security-advisories
WebAssembly runtime Wasmtime addresses security vulnerabilities with broader implications for sandboxed execution
The Bytecode Alliance's transparency on Wasmtime security advisories matters because WASM is becoming infrastructure for edge computing and plugin architectures. Companies building on WASM runtimes (Cloudflare, Fastly, Shopify) depend on this security posture.

## 💼 Business & Market Signals

### ChatGPT finally offers $100/month Pro plan
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/09/chatgpt-pro-plan-100-month-codex/
OpenAI fills the gap between $20 consumer and $200 power user tiers
The 5x price jump from Plus to Pro was leaving money on the table. This $100 tier targets prosumers and small teams—likely the highest-margin segment. Watch subscriber mix shift in OpenAI's next funding round disclosures.

### Google and Intel deepen AI infrastructure partnership
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/09/google-and-intel-deepen-ai-infrastructure-partnership/
Custom chip co-development signals Intel's pivot to foundry services is gaining traction
Google choosing Intel for custom AI silicon co-development is a meaningful vote of confidence as Intel fights for relevance against TSMC. The "global shortage" framing suggests hyperscalers are locking in capacity ahead of further supply constraints—capex commitments worth monitoring.

### After data breach, $10B-valued startup Mercor is having a month
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/09/after-data-breach-10b-valued-startup-mercor-is-having-a-month/
Enterprise customer churn following security incidents tests unicorn resilience
Mercor's situation is a live case study in how quickly enterprise value erodes post-breach. Lawsuits plus reported customer departures could pressure the $10B valuation in any secondary transactions. Due diligence on late-stage HR tech should weight security posture heavily.

### Amazon CEO takes aim at Nvidia, Intel, Starlink, more in annual shareholder letter
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/09/amazon-ceo-takes-aim-at-nvidia-intel-starlink-more-in-annual-shareholder-letter/
Jassy justifies $200B capex by positioning AWS against every infrastructure competitor
The letter reads as a strategic roadmap: Trainium chips vs. Nvidia, Kuiper vs. Starlink, Graviton vs. Intel. Amazon's vertical integration ambitions are explicit now. Suppliers in their crosshairs should expect margin pressure.

## 📋 Case Studies & Deep Dives

### What founders can learn from Anjuna's layoffs and recovery
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/09/what-founders-can-learn-from-anjunas-layoffs-and-recovery/
Confidential computing startup's 2021-2022 contraction offers playbook for right-sizing
Anjuna scaled to 75 employees anticipating hypergrowth that didn't materialize. The recovery narrative is useful pattern recognition for evaluating other enterprise security startups that over-hired during ZIRP. Key question: did the core technology thesis survive the restructuring?

### Is Anthropic limiting the release of Mythos to protect the internet — or Anthropic?
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/09/is-anthropic-limiting-the-release-of-mythos-to-protect-the-internet-or-anthropic/
Capability overhang meets regulatory strategy in Anthropic's Mythos release decision
Anthropic claims Mythos finds software exploits too effectively for unrestricted release. The cynical read: they're managing liability and positioning for favorable regulation. Either way, this sets precedent for how frontier labs will gate future capability releases.

### Instant 1.0, a backend for AI-coded apps
URL: https://www.instantdb.com/essays/architecture
Infrastructure play targeting the vibe-coding generation of developers
InstantDB's 1.0 architecture essay positions them as the backend for AI-generated applications. If AI coding tools continue proliferating, simplified backend services become picks-and-shovels plays. The question is whether Firebase, Supabase, or incumbents respond before Instant gains distribution.

## ⚡ Signal vs. Noise

The through-line this week is infrastructure hardening meeting AI acceleration. Google-Intel, Amazon's capex defense, and Anthropic's capability gating all point to the same reality: the easy phase of AI deployment is ending. The next twelve months will separate companies with defensible infrastructure positions from those renting their competitive advantage. Meanwhile, the Mercor breach and OpenAI's Florida investigation remind us that regulatory and security risks are no longer tail events—they're operating expenses.
